name: Item-Based Cold-Start Elicitation (show selection)
core_claim: Item-based cold-start elicitation is the industry-standard onboarding pattern: show a new user a set of seed items (titles, posters, trailers) and ask them to pick/rate the ones they like, then infer a preference vector from those choices to seed recommendations before any organic viewing history exists. Netflix's classic 'pick 3 titles you like' signup step is the canonical example; Spotify, Pandora, and most streaming services use a variant. Theoretically it is not a measurement of any latent psychological construct — it directly samples revealed preference over the actual catalogue, then maps the chosen seeds into the same collaborative-filtering / embedding space used to rank everything else. The implicit claim is that a handful of liked items locates the user in item-embedding space well enough to bootstrap useful recommendations. Its strength over personality instruments is that it elicits exactly the variable being predicted (item preference), not a weak proxy for it; its weakness is that it presupposes the user recognises and can react to the seed items, which fails precisely for new users, niche catalogues, and cross-medium inference. It is the natural comparison baseline against which personality-based and other content-light cold-start methods must justify themselves.
predictive_validity_for_media: This is the highest-ceiling cold-start signal because it elicits revealed item preference directly rather than a proxy. In offline evaluation, active-learning item-selection strategies (popularity, coverage/co-rating, uncertainty-reduction) measurably improve early recommendation accuracy versus a non-personalised popular-items prior, and personalised/adaptive (2-phase 'burn-in then adaptive') elicitation outperforms a fixed seed set because no single seed set is optimal across diverse users (Cold-start Recommendation by Personalized Embedding Region Elicitation, arXiv:2406.00973, 2024). Crucially the construct-to-target gap is near-zero: 5-10 liked titles predict GENRE, THEMATIC, and NARRATIVE-STYLE preference far better than any Big Five trait (which top out around r~0.2-0.3 for Openness on complex content) because the seeds already encode genre+theme+style jointly. The major empirical caveat is the offline/online gap: active-learning gains demonstrated offline have repeatedly FAILED to replicate in online studies with real users, because real users cannot rate many of the algorithmically-selected items (they have not seen them), so the requested signal is never supplied (Active learning algorithm for alleviating the user cold start problem, Scientific Reports 2025, s41598-025-09708-2; corroborated across the active-learning literature). COMPLETION-RATE prediction is not directly addressed by item-pick elicitation and should be treated as unsupported until interaction data accrues. Net: best-available cold-start ranker when the user recognises the seeds; degrades sharply when they do not.
instrument_design: The 'instrument' is a catalogue-grounded selection task, not a psychometric scale, so item count is a product choice rather than a validity constraint. Canonical form: a grid of N posters (Netflix historically ~visual title grid, user taps ~3+; many systems show 20-50 and ask for 3-10 selections), binary like / multi-select, sometimes a 'show me more' refresh. Response formats: (a) multi-select pick ('choose titles you like') — lowest friction, the dominant pattern; (b) per-item Likert/thumbs rating — more signal per item, higher friction; (c) pairwise / choice-based ('which of these two?') — reduces scale-use bias and can be gamified; (d) 2-phase adaptive — fixed popular burn-in set then sequentially-selected adaptive items (best offline performance, more screens). Selection heuristics for the seed pool: popularity (recognisable but redundant, low information), coverage/co-rating (items diagnostic of many others), and uncertainty-reduction/entropy (maximally informative but often unrecognised). MINIMUM VIABLE under-2-min / 6-10 'item' version: a single screen of ~24-36 recognisable, genre-and-theme-diverse posters, multi-select, asking for >=3 picks, completable in well under a minute. The hard design tension: information-optimal items (niche, entropy-maximising) are exactly the ones new users cannot recognise, so a low-friction version must lean on recognisable popular+diverse seeds and accept lower per-item information.
cold_start_applicability: This IS the prototypical cold-start method, so applicability is definitional for single-medium recognised catalogues, but it has two structural blind spots that motivate complementary signals. (1) Recognition dependency: it produces zero signal from items the user has not heard of, so for users new to a medium/region (exactly HelpME2C's cross-medium and multilingual case) the recognisable seed pool collapses to globally-popular Western titles and the inferred vector is biased toward the mainstream. (2) Cross-medium inference is its weakest axis: liking specific TV titles does not cleanly transfer to anime (or vice-versa) without a learned bridge. Cross-domain recommendation research explicitly treats TV->anime as a transfer problem requiring a mapping/bridge function learned from overlapping users (Transfer-Meta Framework, arXiv:2105.04785; Sharpness-Aware CDR arXiv:2408.01931), and such bridges themselves need source-domain history — which a true cold-start user lacks. So item-based elicitation gives excellent same-medium, same-catalogue signal at t=0 and progressively weaker signal as you move to (a) media the user hasn't consumed, (b) the other medium, and (c) niche/foreign titles. This is precisely the gap a content-light prior (personality, theme-values, mood) is positioned to fill, because such priors are catalogue-agnostic and transfer across media by construction.
group_compatibility_use: For the HelpME2C two-person core, item-based elicitation extends naturally and arguably more interpretably than personality vectors, because the unit being combined (item preferences) is the unit being recommended. Two collected seed sets can be aggregated at three stages (rating aggregation, result aggregation, or group-model aggregation; standard GRS taxonomy): (1) INTERSECTION of inferred preference vectors / overlapping liked seeds — finds shared taste, strongest joint-satisfaction signal but sparse for dissimilar pairs; (2) AVERAGE of the two embedding vectors — maximises mean predicted satisfaction, can recommend titles neither would pick alone; (3) LEAST-MISERY — rank by the minimum of the two predicted scores, which the dyad literature favours because a single dissatisfied member in a two-person group is highly salient and cannot be diluted by a majority. A concrete dyad-friendly UX variant is JOINT elicitation: show seeds and let both partners react in one session, capturing explicit agreement/disagreement per title rather than reconciling two separate profiles post-hoc. The key advantage over personality aggregation: a shared liked title is direct evidence of joint watchability, whereas combining two Openness scores is an indirect inference. The key weakness: cold-start dyads often have little catalogue overlap, so intersection is sparse and the system falls back to popularity, re-importing the mainstream bias. Direct empirical evidence that a specific aggregation rule maximises measured JOINT viewing satisfaction for two-person dyads remains thin; treat the rule as an A/B-testable hyperparameter. [uncertain]
quiz_ux_implications: As an onboarding UX this is the most consumer-proven cold-start pattern (Netflix's 3-pick step is studied as a conversion-optimised onboarding flow; UserOnboarding.Academy / Zemoga case studies), so direct elicitation is appropriate and needs no disguising — picking shows you like is intrinsically motivating and low-reactance because it visibly feeds the product's value. It largely sidesteps the psychometric biases that plague personality scales: there is no acquiescence-bias problem (no agree/disagree statements), low social-desirability pressure (choosing entertainment is low-stakes), and no faking incentive. However it introduces DIFFERENT biases. (a) SELECTION / POPULARITY bias is the dominant threat: which seed items you show determines which preferences you can even observe; users disproportionately pick recognisable popular titles, the system learns mainstream taste, recommends more mainstream, and a feedback loop entrenches popularity bias and starves niche/new items (A First Look at Selection Bias in Preference Elicitation, arXiv:2405.00554, RecSys CONSEQUENCES 2023; A Survey on Popularity Bias, arXiv:2308.01118). (b) RECOGNITION bias: absence of a pick is ambiguous — dislike or never-heard-of-it — confounding the signal. Mitigations: show a deliberately genre/theme-diverse seed grid (not just top-popular), use visual posters/trailers to make even unfamiliar titles evaluable (trailers shown to reduce popularity bias in choice-based elicitation, Can Trailers Help..., ResearchGate 308173264), offer a 'show me different ones' refresh, and treat non-selection as missing-not-disliked. Friction-minimising format: single-screen poster multi-select, >=3 picks, with an explicit skip.
cross_cultural_validity: Item-based elicitation has no psychometric cross-cultural validity to defend (it is not a scale), but it has a sharper practical cross-cultural failure mode than personality instruments: the seed catalogue is itself culturally loaded. A globally-popular seed grid is overwhelmingly Western/English-dominant, so a Korean, Japanese, Hindi, or Arabic-first user is shown titles they may not recognise, picks few or defaults to the handful of global hits, and is mislocated toward Western mainstream taste — directly degrading HelpME2C's multilingual value proposition. Unlike a personality score (catalogue-agnostic, transfers across cultures modulo measurement-invariance caveats), the quality of item-based signal is a direct function of catalogue/region match. This is fixable but only by engineering: region- and language-aware seed selection (show K-drama and anime seeds to relevant users, localise the poster grid), which requires knowing the user's locale before elicitation and maintaining per-region recognisable seed pools. Without that, the method systematically underserves exactly the non-Western, non-English users HelpME2C's multilingual catalogue is meant to serve. The cross-cultural risk is therefore real and operational rather than psychometric. [uncertain]
privacy_consent_implications: Lower-risk than personality profiling. The data collected is declared content preference (titles liked), which is ordinary personal data, not GDPR Article 9 special-category data on its face. The caveat: liked titles can be a vector for inferring special-category attributes (e.g. religious, political, sexual-orientation content choices can imply protected characteristics), so if HelpME2C ever intentionally derives such inferences the data tips toward Article 9 (ICO 'intentional inference' test) — but for entertainment ranking the intended use stays ordinary. Consent: the action is self-evidently purposeful (the user is visibly tuning their own recommendations), supporting clear, freely-given consent at the point of collection; still requires a purpose statement and must be deletable/hard-deleted per ADR-0012 DSAR rules. Re-identification risk is modest (a list of liked shows is less uniquely identifying than a clinical trait vector, though long preference histories can be fingerprinting). Net: easier consent story and lower sensitive-inference risk than personality instruments, but still touches privacy-control flows (CLAUDE.md §4) so confirm before building. Minimise by storing the derived prior where the raw pick list is not needed.
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: Item-based elicitation maps to the theme vocabulary INDIRECTLY but RICHLY, and this is its structural advantage over personality: each picked title is already tagged (in HelpME2C's taxonomy) with concrete theme slugs, so a liked-title set yields a direct empirical distribution over theme slugs rather than a reasoned extrapolation from a trait. If a user picks Dark, Breaking Bad, and Better Call Saul, the system reads off high weight on moral-compromise, power-corrupts, guilt-and-atonement, rise-and-fall, vigilante-justice, complicity-with-evil directly from those titles' theme tags. Picking Studio Ghibli + slice-of-life anime reads off found-family, coming-of-age, meditation-on-time, grief-and-loss, slow-burn. This is the inverse mapping direction to personality work: personality predicts theme preference probabilistically (high Openness -> nonlinear-storytelling, earned-ambiguity), whereas item-picks reveal theme preference observationally via the tags of chosen items. The practical implication for HelpME2C: item-based elicitation is the natural way to POPULATE the theme-preference vector at cold start when the user recognises the seeds, and the theme vocabulary is the bridge that lets a TV pick transfer to anime recommendations (shared theme slugs cross the medium boundary even when titles don't). The limitation re-emerges where it always does: a user who picks nothing (unrecognised seeds) yields an empty theme distribution, which is exactly where a personality/values prior must supply the initial theme weights instead.
implementation_recommendation: INCLUDE as the PRIMARY cold-start elicitation mechanism, with a content-light prior (personality/values/mood) as a COMPLEMENT that fills its blind spots — not as a competitor. Recommended design: a single-screen, region/language-aware poster grid of ~24-36 genre- and theme-diverse titles, multi-select, ask for >=3 picks, posters/trailers to make even unfamiliar titles evaluable, an explicit 'show me different ones' refresh, and a skip. Read theme-slug distributions off the tags of picked titles to seed the theme-preference vector directly (this is the cleanest path to populate the theme vocabulary at t=0). Treat non-selection as missing-not-disliked. For the two-person core: support a JOINT elicitation session (both partners react to one seed set) and store both vectors; aggregate via least-misery or intersection as an A/B-testable hyperparameter; use shared liked titles as the strongest joint-watchability signal. CRITICAL DEPENDENCIES / why it cannot stand alone for HelpME2C: (1) cross-medium transfer is weak — lean on the theme vocabulary as the bridge between TV and anime picks; (2) cross-cultural/multilingual coverage requires region-aware seed pools or it underserves non-Western users; (3) recognition dependency means a personality/values prior should be available as fallback when the user recognises few seeds; (4) actively counter selection/popularity bias in seed choice (diversify the grid, use trailers) or the recommender entrenches mainstream taste via a feedback loop. North-Star trace: 'a quick joint show-pick at signup gives both partners a shared, theme-grounded preference seed so their first joint recommendation is plausibly watchable before any viewing history exists' — directly traceable. This is the baseline every other cold-start signal in this research should be benchmarked against.

(2024), 'Cold-start Recommendation by Personalized Embedding Region Elicitation' — free: https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.00973 (2-phase burn-in+adaptive elicitation, fixed-vs-personalised seed sets).
